phases.label: Voter Registration
phases.description: The first step to participating in democracy. Ensure your name is on the electoral roll.
steps: Check name in Voter List via Voter Service Portal

requirements: Must be a registered voter in any constituency

phases.label: Candidate Nomination
phases.description: Candidates file their papers to contest the election.
steps: Filing of nomination papers with Returning Officer
voterActions: Check candidate affidavits on ECI site

candidateDetails.disqualification: Failure to lodge account of election expenses
documents: Certified copy of electoral roll entry

phases.label: Campaigning
phases.description: Political parties and candidates reach out to voters with their manifestos.
voterActions: Report MCC violations via cVIGIL app

mccRules: No appeal to caste or communal feelings
No use of religious places for election propaganda
candidateDetails.permits: Required for loudspeakers, rallies, and vehicles

phases.label: Polling Day
phases.description: The day you cast your vote using EVMs and VVPATs.
voterActions: Carry Voter ID or approved alternative ID
Check the blue button on EVM

phases.label: Counting & Results
phases.description: Votes are counted and results are declared.
voterActions: Watch live results on ECI website

glossary.definition: The lower house of India's bicameral Parliament, known as the 'House of the People.' It has 543 directly elected seats. The party or coalition with a majority forms the government.
The upper house of Parliament, known as the 'Council of States.' It has 245 members — 233 elected by state legislative assemblies and 12 nominated by the President.
The autonomous constitutional body established under Article 324 that administers all election processes in India.
A standalone electronic device used to cast and count votes in Indian elections. Each EVM consists of a Control Unit and a Ballot Unit.
Voter Verified Paper Audit Trail — a printer device attached to EVMs that generates a paper slip showing the voter's choice, visible for 7 seconds.
Guidelines issued by the ECI to regulate political parties and candidates during elections. Comes into effect when election dates are announced.
A geographic area whose residents elect one representative to Parliament or a state legislature. India has 543 Lok Sabha constituencies.
An official appointed by the ECI to oversee the election process in a constituency — from receiving nominations to declaring results.
'None of the Above' — an option on the EVM ballot allowing voters to reject all candidates. Introduced in 2013 following the Supreme Court's PUCL judgment.
The process of redrawing electoral constituency boundaries based on census data, carried out by the Delimitation Commission.
A sworn legal declaration every candidate must file disclosing criminal record, assets, liabilities, and educational qualifications.
The 10th Schedule of the Constitution (1985) which disqualifies an MP/MLA who votes against their party whip or voluntarily gives up membership.
Direct Central Rule under Article 356, imposed when a state government cannot function constitutionally. State legislature is suspended.
An election held to fill a seat that has become vacant mid-term due to death, resignation, or disqualification of a sitting member.
A government official assigned to a specific polling booth responsible for maintaining the electoral roll and assisting in voter registration.
A facility allowing armed forces personnel, police on election duty, and voters with disabilities to vote by post without visiting a polling booth.
One-third of Rajya Sabha members retire every two years, with elections held by state legislative assemblies using the Single Transferable Vote method.
A party recognized by the ECI based on electoral performance. Classification: National (recognized in 4+ states) or State parties.
Senior IAS/IPS officers deployed by the ECI to monitor election conduct and ensure the Model Code of Conduct is followed.
The 48-hour period before polls close during which no political campaigning is permitted, giving voters time to reflect.
84 Lok Sabha seats reserved for SC candidates and 47 for ST candidates. Only members of these communities can contest, though all voters vote.
The Electoral Photo Identity Card issued by the ECI. The ECI also accepts 11 alternative documents including Aadhaar and passport.
For Lok Sabha elections, the limit is ₹95 lakh per candidate. Violation is grounds for disqualification.
The primary legislation governing elections in India — covers qualification/disqualification, election offences, corrupt practices, and dispute resolution.
The President issues election notifications, invites the majority party leader to form government, and administers the oath of office to the PM.

Informal term for the practice of political parties attempting to poach elected members from rival parties through inducements — often money or ministerial positions. The Anti-Defection Law (10th Schedule) was introduced specifically to curb this practice.

quickQuestions: What documents do I need to vote?
How to check my name in the voter list?
Can I vote if I am an NRI?

quiz.question: What is the minimum age to vote in Indian general elections?
quiz.explanation: The 61st Constitutional Amendment (1988) lowered the voting age from 21 to 18 years.
quiz.funFact: India has more voters under the age of 25 than the entire population of the United States.

quiz.question: Which body is responsible for conducting elections in India?
quiz.explanation: The Election Commission of India (ECI) is an autonomous constitutional authority established on January 25, 1950.
quiz.funFact: January 25 is celebrated as National Voters' Day in India — the anniversary of the ECI's founding.

quiz.question: What does 'Lok Sabha' literally mean?
quiz.explanation: 'Lok Sabha' translates to 'House of the People' in Hindi. It is the lower house with 543 elected seats.
quiz.funFact: The first Lok Sabha session was held on May 13, 1952. The youngest-ever MP was Chandrani Murmu, elected at age 25.

quiz.question: What is NOTA in Indian elections?
options: None of the Above option on ballot
quiz.explanation: NOTA was introduced in 2013 following a Supreme Court order, allowing voters to reject all candidates.
quiz.funFact: In the 2019 general election, over 6.5 million voters chose NOTA — more than the population of Singapore.

quiz.question: How many seats does a party need to win a majority in the Lok Sabha?
quiz.explanation: The Lok Sabha has 543 elected seats. A simple majority requires 272 seats (more than half).
quiz.funFact: The largest-ever Lok Sabha majority was in 1984, when the INC won 414 of 543 seats after Indira Gandhi's assassination.

quiz.question: What is the security deposit amount for a Lok Sabha candidate?
quiz.explanation: A candidate must deposit ₹25,000 (₹12,500 for SC/ST). Forfeited if they fail to secure 1/6th of valid votes.
quiz.funFact: In the 2019 elections, over 80% of independent candidates lost their security deposits — the deposit is designed to deter frivolous candidacies.

quiz.question: What is the Model Code of Conduct (MCC)?
options: Guidelines issued by ECI for parties and candidates during elections
quiz.explanation: The MCC regulates parties and candidates during elections, active from date announcement to result declaration.
quiz.funFact: The MCC is not a law — it's a voluntary code. But parties follow it because the ECI can postpone elections in a constituency if it's violated.

quiz.question: What is VVPAT and what is its purpose?
options: A paper trail that lets voters verify their EVM vote
A postal ballot system for armed forces
quiz.explanation: VVPAT prints a slip showing the candidate voted for, visible for 7 seconds, providing an audit trail.
quiz.funFact: In 2019, the Supreme Court ordered VVPAT verification of 5 randomly selected EVMs per constituency — requiring over 20,000 VVPAT audits.

quiz.question: Which Article of the Indian Constitution establishes the Election Commission?
quiz.explanation: Article 324 vests the superintendence and control of all elections in the Election Commission of India.
quiz.funFact: Sukumar Sen, the first Chief Election Commissioner, organized India's first-ever election in 1951-52 — covering 173 million voters, most of whom couldn't read.

quiz.question: Why does India conduct elections in multiple phases?
options: To allow candidates to campaign in multiple constituencies
To deploy adequate security forces across a vast geography
quiz.explanation: Multi-phase elections (up to 7) allow adequate deployment of central security forces across 543 constituencies.
quiz.funFact: The 2019 general election lasted 39 days across 7 phases and cost approximately ₹60,000 crore — making it the most expensive election in the world at that time.

quiz.question: Under which provision can the President of India dissolve the Lok Sabha?
quiz.explanation: Under Article 85(2)(b), the President can dissolve the Lok Sabha on the advice of the Prime Minister.
quiz.funFact: The Lok Sabha has been dissolved prematurely 8 times — most recently in 1999 when the Vajpayee government lost a confidence motion by just 1 vote.

quiz.question: What is the Representation of the People Act, 1951 primarily concerned with?
options: Regulating the actual conduct of elections and qualifications of representatives
quiz.explanation: The RPA 1951 governs conduct of elections, qualification/disqualification, corrupt practices, and election disputes.
quiz.funFact: Section 123 of the RPA defines 'corrupt practices' — including hiring vehicles to transport voters to polling stations, which is still illegal today.

quiz.question: What happens if a Lok Sabha candidate's security deposit is forfeited?
options: They are banned from future elections
The ₹25,000 deposit is retained by the government
The result of the constituency is annulled
The candidate must pay a penalty
quiz.explanation: If a candidate fails to secure 1/6th of valid votes, the deposit is forfeited to the Consolidated Fund of India.
quiz.funFact: In 2019, the ECI collected over ₹47 crore in forfeited deposits — mostly from independent candidates who got very few votes.

quiz.question: What is 'delimitation' in the context of Indian elections?
options: The fixing of boundaries and seats of electoral constituencies
quiz.explanation: Delimitation redraws constituency boundaries based on census data. The current freeze is due for review after 2026.
quiz.funFact: The 2002 Delimitation Commission took 6 years to complete its work. The freeze on delimitation (based on 1971 population data) was meant to encourage family planning.

quiz.question: Under the anti-defection law (10th Schedule), when can an MP vote against their party without losing their seat?
options: Never — any vote against the party results in disqualification
Only if more than 2/3 of the party's legislature members support the dissent
Only with written permission from the Speaker
If the vote is on a private member's bill
quiz.explanation: An MP is not disqualified if at least 2/3 of the party's legislative members vote in favour of a merger.
quiz.funFact: The 10th Schedule was introduced in 1985 after the 'Aaya Ram Gaya Ram' era of the 1960s-70s, when one MLA in Haryana switched parties 3 times in a single day.
